preFix.title: Setting up a dairy goat milking routine
preFix.subtitle: Twice-daily milking, the milking stand, hygiene, and what to expect in the first week.
preFix.excerpt: A dairy goat in milk needs twice-daily milking at consistent intervals, or yield drops and mastitis risk rises. Before the first kid is weaned and the milking routine begins, the setup needs to be ready: a secure milking stand, clean equipment, and a sequence that protects milk quality and keeps the doe settled.
preFix.sourceNotes: AHDB Dairy Cattle guidance adapted for goats (ahdb.org.uk). Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, dairy goat chapters. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Goats, 2019.

content.text: A dairy goat's lactation runs from kidding for around 9-10 months if she is milked consistently. The foundation of good yield and healthy udder is a routine that never varies: the same times, the same sequence, the same person where possible. Goats settle into a milking pattern quickly and become increasingly easy to work with as the routine becomes familiar.

before the first milking. A plywood platform 90 cm x 45 cm with a simple latch head-gate is sufficient; the platform should be at a height where you can sit on a low stool to reach the udder without bending. The head-gate holds the doe while she eats a small handful of concentrate feed, which should last exactly the length of a milking. A goat that finishes her feed before you are done will start fidgeting.
Follow the same sequence every milking. Bring the doe to the stand, secure the head-gate, add her feed, wipe the udder with a warm damp cloth. Carry out a
of 3-4 streams from each teat into a strip cup or onto the floor, observing for any abnormality. Then milk each teat fully, alternating sides to encourage let-down. Finish by applying

Twice-daily milking should be at 12-hour intervals. Morning and evening at the same times each day maintains yield and reduces the risk of mastitis from overfull quarters. A doe that is not milked for more than 14 hours is at risk of milk accumulating in the udder and fermenting. In a practical smallholding routine, 06:00 and 18:00 are common; any pair that fits the keeper's schedule works as long as it is consistent.

Rinse milking equipment in cold water first (not hot, which denatures protein and makes cleaning harder), then wash with hot water and dairy detergent, then rinse. Allow to air dry inverted. Milk for human consumption from a small dairy goat holding falls within the rules for raw milk sale only if registered with the Food Standards Agency; for personal use there is no licensing requirement, but the same hygiene standards apply.
